A-006-001-001: Converting every incoming signal down to one fixed intermediate frequency (IF) lets the designer build the high-gain, sharply tuned IF filters once and optimize them perfectly, instead of trying to make tuned circuits track sharply across the whole tuning range. That fixed IF is where the selectivity comes from. The distractors mix in unrelated functions like squelch, detection or limiting that are not what the frequency-conversion step provides.
A-006-001-002: Picking the IF is mostly about keeping unwanted responses out: the image frequency sits 2×IF away from the wanted signal, so a higher IF pushes the image far enough that the front-end can reject it, while still being low enough for good IF selectivity. So image rejection and other spurious responses dominate the choice. Noise figure, cross-modulation and interference to others are set by different parts of the receiver, not by the IF value.
A-006-001-003: In double conversion the first IF is made high so the image frequency lands far away and is easily filtered, then a second conversion drops to a low IF for sharp selectivity. This gives much better image rejection for the same front-end tuned-circuit quality than single conversion can. The other options (stability, sensitivity, loudness) are not what the extra conversion buys you.
A-006-001-004: A crystal filter provides the narrow, sharp-skirted selectivity a communications receiver needs, and that selectivity is set at the fixed intermediate frequency, so the crystal filter lives in the IF circuits. Crystals are cut for one frequency, which is why they suit the fixed IF and not the tunable local oscillator. The detector and audio stages handle the signal after selectivity has already been established.
A-006-001-005: Each extra oscillator and mixer in a multiple-conversion design creates more sum-and-difference products, and any of those mixing combinations can let an unwanted signal sneak through as a spurious response. More frequencies being multiplied together means more chances for spurs. The problem is the added mixing products, not poorer IF selectivity, higher sensitivity, or AGC overload.
A-006-001-006: The first conversion uses a high IF so the image frequency is far off and easy to reject, giving image rejection; the second conversion drops to a low IF where narrow filters give the fine selectivity. So the order is image rejection first, then selectivity. The reversed answer (selectivity then image rejection) is the tempting trap because both words are correct but the sequence is backwards.
A-006-001-007: The RF amplifier is the stage that handles the actual received signal, so both its input and output tuned circuits are peaked at the incoming frequency to amplify it while rejecting off-channel signals. The local oscillator runs at a different frequency (offset by the IF), and the detector and audio stages work at the IF or audio, not the received frequency.
A-006-001-008: The mixer takes the tunable input (RF/preselector following the variable local oscillator) on one side and feeds the fixed-tuned IF amplifier on the other, so it sits between a tunable stage and a fixed-tuned stage. That is exactly the mixer's job, to translate the variable input frequency to the fixed IF. The RF amp is before the mixer, the IF amp after it, and the local oscillator is the tunable source, not the in-between stage.
A-006-001-009: The mixer produces sum and difference frequencies, so the received signal is either LO − IF or LO + IF. Here 16 MHz − 9 MHz = 7 MHz (the low-side answer); the high-side image would be 16 + 9 = 25 MHz. Of the choices given, 7 MHz is the frequency the receiver is tuned to.
A-006-001-010: Double conversion means two mixing steps, so there are two IF stages and two local oscillators, one for each conversion. The beat-frequency oscillator is a separate, additional oscillator used only to recover SSB, so it is mentioned on top of those two. Counting only one IF/one LO ignores the second conversion, and three local oscillators wrongly counts the BFO as a conversion oscillator.
A-006-001-011: Making the first IF high moves the image frequency far from the wanted signal, so even a simple front end rejects the image well; a double-conversion receiver therefore suffers less image interference than a single-conversion set of the same sensitivity. The benefit is image rejection — not curing drift, raising sensitivity, or louder audio.
A-006-002-001: The mixer beats the incoming RF signal against the local oscillator and outputs the difference (the intermediate frequency), so the signal can then be handled by the fixed IF amplifiers. Changing the incoming frequency to the IF is the mixer's whole purpose. It does not remove images, allow multiple IFs, or make audio; those happen elsewhere.
A-006-002-002: SSB transmits only one sideband with the carrier suppressed, and the detector needs that carrier to recover the audio, so the BFO reinjects a locally generated carrier to take its place. Without it the SSB signal demodulates as unintelligible. The BFO does not phase out a sideband, narrow the IF, or create a missing sideband; it simply supplies the missing carrier reference.
A-006-002-003: Mixing the incoming signal with the local oscillator produces sum and difference products, and the receiver uses the difference, which is the fixed intermediate frequency. That IF is what the following selective amplifier stages are tuned to. It is not an audio frequency (that comes after detection) nor just a generic RF.
A-006-002-004: The LO sits one IF away from the signal, so LO = signal ± IF. With signal 3600 kHz and IF 9 MHz = 9000 kHz: 3600 + 9000 = 12 600 kHz (high-side) or 9000 − 3600 = 5400 kHz (low-side). Of the listed choices, 5400 kHz is correct; note 21 600 kHz comes from wrongly adding 3600 to 18 000.
A-006-002-005: For CW and SSB the detector needs a tone, so the BFO is offset by a few hundred hertz to a couple of kilohertz; beating the offset BFO against the incoming signal produces an audible difference tone (the 500–1500 Hz beat note). Without that offset there would be zero-beat and no audible tone. It is not there to amplify or protect the signal.
A-006-002-006: Receiver oscillators must be stable so the tuning does not drift off the signal, and spectrally pure so they do not generate extra frequencies that create spurious responses or reciprocal mixing. Sensitivity and selectivity are properties of amplifier and filter stages, not of the oscillators themselves, so those options confuse oscillator requirements with receiver performance terms.
A-006-002-007: The variable capacitor with its trimmer and inductor forms the resonant circuit that sets the oscillator frequency, and since the LO must tune across the band to keep the difference at the fixed IF, this is the local-oscillator tuning. The BFO runs near the fixed IF and does not tune across the band, so the answers naming the BFO are wrong.
A-006-002-008: Without an RF amplifier the variable capacitor and inductor at the mixer input form the preselector tuned circuit, peaked at the wanted reception frequency to keep images and off-channel signals out of the mixer. That is the preselector tuning the receiver to the reception frequency. The BFO and LO are separate oscillators, not this input resonant circuit.
A-006-002-009: The stage that combines an incoming signal with an oscillator to produce a different output frequency is the mixer: 14.25 MHz − 13.795 MHz = 0.455 MHz = 455 kHz, exactly the IF. A BFO works at the IF for detection, a VFO is just an oscillator, and a multiplier raises a frequency by a whole number, so only the mixer does this frequency translation.
A-006-002-010: Before mixing, the RF amplifier and the first mixer both have input tuned circuits peaked at the wanted received frequency so they pass the same signal. The local oscillator is deliberately offset by the IF, and the IF amplifier works at the fixed IF, so those stages are tuned to different frequencies, not the received one.
A-006-002-011: The mixer's intended job is to combine the incoming signal with the local oscillator and output the intermediate frequency for the IF stages. Spurious signals are an unwanted side effect of mixing, not its purpose, so 'produces spurious signals' is the trap; the mixer is not a buffer and does not by itself demodulate SSB.
A-006-003-001: The noise floor is the level of the receiver's own internally generated noise, so the weakest signal you can actually detect is one that just rises above that noise. A signal buried below the noise floor cannot be distinguished. It is about internal receiver noise, not atmospheric conditions, RF overload, or just the local oscillator's noise alone.
A-006-003-002: The first IF amplifier provides much of the receiver's gain and is where the selective filtering lives, so its purpose is to improve both selectivity and gain. Noise figure is set mainly by the RF/front-end stages, not the IF, and the IF does not tune out cross-modulation, so those options misplace functions that belong elsewhere.
A-006-003-003: The RF amplifier should have just enough gain to lift weak signals above the noise the first mixer generates, because the mixer is a relatively noisy stage. Too little gain leaves signals below mixer noise; too much gain (the tempting 'as much as possible' answer) overloads the mixer and hurts dynamic range. So the goal is enough gain to overcome mixer noise, no more.
A-006-003-004: The RF amplifier's main value is that it amplifies the signal before the noisy mixer, setting a low overall noise figure and thus good sensitivity. Most of the receiver's gain actually comes from the IF stages, not the RF amp, so 'provide most of the gain' is the tempting but wrong choice; the RF amp's real payoff is noise figure.
A-006-003-005: UHF FM receiver sensitivity is commonly quoted as the RF input level needed to reach 12 dB SINAD, a standard measure of when the recovered audio is sufficiently above noise and distortion. BER is used for digital modes, noise figure and gain are different specs, so 12 dB SINAD is the conventional FM sensitivity figure.
A-006-003-006: Dynamic range is the decibel span between the strongest signal the receiver can handle without producing audible distortion and the weakest detectable signal (its sensitivity). It captures how wide a range of signal strengths the receiver copes with at once. Stability, noise figure and 'design parameter' describe other properties, not this ratio.
A-006-003-007: Noise figure measures how much noise the receiver adds, so a lower noise figure means weaker signals stay above the noise and can be heard, which is greater sensitivity. Selectivity and rejection are set by filters, and stability by oscillators, so only sensitivity tracks the noise figure.
A-006-003-008: Noise contributes most where the signal is still weak and being amplified at the front, so in a good design the dominant internal noise comes from the RF amplifier and the mixer. By the time the signal reaches the detector and audio stages it is already strong, so noise added there matters far less.
A-006-003-009: On HF the external noise picked up by the antenna, both natural (atmospheric) and man-made, is far larger than the receiver's own internal noise, so a very low noise figure brings no benefit, the external noise dominates anyway. This is why ultra-low-noise front ends matter at VHF/UHF but not on HF. The other options misattribute the cause to ionospheric effects, modulation type, or later stages.
A-006-003-010: Dynamic range is specifically the span of signal amplitudes, from the weakest detectable to the strongest tolerable, that the receiver can handle simultaneously. AGC and noise figure relate to gain control and sensitivity rather than this amplitude span, so dynamic range is the correct term for accommodating multiple signal levels.
A-006-003-011: The resonant tuned circuits before and after the RF stage, which select the wanted frequency and reject images and off-channel signals, are together called the preselector. They pre-select the band of interest ahead of the mixer. Preamplifier refers only to a gain stage, and preamble/pass-selector are not real receiver terms.
A-006-004-001: FM transmitters boost (pre-emphasize) the higher audio frequencies before transmission, so the receiver must apply the opposite, a de-emphasis network, to attenuate the highs and restore the original balance, which proportionally brings the lower frequencies back up. Pre-emphasis is what the transmitter does; using it again at the receiver would double the distortion instead of correcting it.
A-006-004-002: A product detector recovers SSB and CW by mixing the incoming IF signal with a locally generated carrier (from the BFO), producing the difference as audio. It is essentially a mixer used for detection. It does not provide oscillations to a mixer, narrow bandpass, or detect cross-modulation; its defining action is multiplying signal by a local carrier.
A-006-004-003: If only strong signals are distorted while weak ones are clean, the problem is the gain-riding circuit that responds to strong signals, namely the AGC, being defective or misadjusted. A fault in the IF, AF or RF amplifier itself would typically distort signals regardless of strength, so the strength-dependent symptom points specifically at the AGC.
A-006-004-004: AGC keeps the audio level roughly constant by sensing signal strength and reducing receiver gain as the signal gets stronger (and raising gain on weak signals). So a stronger signal causes the AGC to reduce gain. Increasing gain would do the opposite of its purpose, and AGC is meant to prevent distortion and limiting, not cause them.
A-006-004-005: After the IF amplifier raises the signal to a usable level, that amplified IF is fed to the detector, which recovers the audio (or other modulation). The RF amplifier and LO come before the IF, and the audio output stage comes after detection, so the detector is the correct next stage.
A-006-004-006: The detector recovers a low-level audio signal that is too weak to drive a speaker, so it is sent to the AF (audio) amplifier for amplification first. Feeding it directly to the speaker would be far too quiet, and it is certainly not grounded or sent back to the RF amplifier.
A-006-004-007: Besides manual volume controls, a receiver holds its output roughly steady automatically with automatic gain control, which adjusts gain to compensate for varying signal strength. Automatic frequency control corrects tuning, not level, and 'inverse gain control' and 'automatic load control' are not the standard receiver output-leveling circuit.
A-006-004-008: AGC works by varying the gain of the early high-gain stages, so the control voltage is fed back to the RF and IF amplifiers where it can throttle gain before the signal grows too large. Applying it to the audio stages would act too late, after detection, so the RF and IF amplifiers are the correct targets.
A-006-004-009: AGC control voltage can be developed either from the IF signal level (IF derived) or from the recovered audio (audio derived), the two standard methods. The other combinations mix in RF- or detector-derived labels that are not the conventional pair, so IF derived or audio derived is correct.
A-006-004-010: An AGC loop is characterized mainly by its threshold (the signal level at which gain reduction begins) and its decay time (how quickly gain recovers after the signal drops). Together these set how the loop responds. Blanking, slope, bandwidth, clipping and hang time describe other circuits, not the basic AGC behaviour.
A-006-004-011: Combining the IF signal with a beat-frequency oscillator to produce audio is exactly what a product detector does, multiplying the two to yield the difference as audio for SSB or CW. AGC controls gain, a VFO is an oscillator, and a power supply provides power, so only the product detector performs this detection.
A-006-005-001: Image rejection depends on filtering out the image frequency before it reaches the mixer, and that selectivity ahead of the mixer is provided by the RF amplifier and preselector. So the front-end preselector sets the image rejection ratio. The IF filter, product detector and AGC act after or apart from the mixing step and cannot remove an image once it has been converted to the IF.
A-006-005-002: Desensitization is the loss of receiver sensitivity when a strong nearby signal drives the front end toward overload, reducing gain available for the wanted weak signal. The strong signal need not be on your frequency, just near it. Cross-modulation transfers modulation between signals and squelch/quieting are different effects, so desensitization is the correct term.
A-006-005-003: Desensitization is caused by strong signals near the received frequency overloading the front end and robbing gain from the wanted signal. Squelch and audio gain settings only affect how much audio you hear; they do not reduce the receiver's RF sensitivity, so the strong nearby signal is the real cause.
A-006-005-004: Adding a cavity (or other sharp filter) ahead of the receiver attenuates the strong off-frequency signal before it can overload the front end, reducing desensitization. Lowering squelch gain or raising bandwidth does not stop the overload, and transmitter audio gain is unrelated, so the cavity filter is the effective fix.
A-006-005-005: Intermodulation arises when two or more signals mix in a nonlinear circuit or device, producing new sum-and-difference products that fall on other frequencies. A perfectly linear stage would not create these products. Too little gain, positive feedback or lack of neutralization cause other problems, so nonlinearity is the root cause of intermod.
A-006-005-006: The image frequency sits 2×IF away from the wanted signal, so using a very high (VHF) IF moves the image far outside the front-end filter passband where it is easily rejected. A higher IF does not widen tuning range or tune out cross-modulation; its key benefit is pushing the image far from the passband.
A-006-005-007: Intermodulation interference is produced when two or more strong signals mix together in the nonlinear front end of the receiver, generating spurious products that land on the wanted frequency. The defining cause is signals mixing in the front end. It is not specifically about transmitter final stages or only the IF amplifiers, where the wanted signal has already been filtered.
A-006-005-008: Instability (drift and unwanted oscillation) comes from things that physically change the tuned circuits, such as temperature variations, mechanical movement, and stray feedback. The accuracy of the dial display only affects how correctly the frequency is shown, not whether the receiver actually stays put, so it is NOT a cause of instability.
A-006-005-009: Frequency stability depends on the oscillators, and the local oscillator (plus the power supply that feeds it) is where drift usually starts, since small voltage or temperature changes shift its frequency and therefore the tuning. The detector, RF amplifier and mixer do not set the tuned frequency, so they are not the usual source of drift.
A-006-005-010: When a strong signal hits a receiver with poor dynamic range, the overloaded nonlinear front end causes desensitization, intermodulation, and cross-modulation. Feedback, by contrast, is a circuit-design or stability issue, not a direct result of a strong signal overloading the front end, so it is the odd one out.
A-006-005-011: In a crowded band the limiting factor is how the receiver handles strong nearby signals, and the two-tone third-order IMD dynamic range with a realistic spacing (here 10 MHz) directly measures resistance to intermodulation from strong out-of-band signals. The third-order intercept point and blocking range are related single specs, but the two-tone IMD dynamic range is the practical performance indicator, while IF rejection ratio addresses a different problem.
